pattern: Keeping the most columns so every row stays non-decreasing is a longest-increasing-subsequence on columns where two columns are compatible only if no row inverts between them.
hints: Deleting the fewest columns is the same as keeping the most columns.
Two kept columns j<i are compatible only if every row has its char at j at most its char at i.
Run an LIS over columns: dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j]) over earlier compatible j; the answer is total columns minus the best dp.

edge_inputs.description: three deletions needed for two rows
keeping non-adjacent columns beats keeping adjacent ones
one row forces deletions the others would not

stress.description: random small-alphabet grids that force nontrivial column LIS
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    rows = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 20)))
    cols = rng.randint(1, 9)
    alpha = 'abcd'
    strs = [''.join(rng.choice(alpha) for _ in range(cols)) for _ in range(rows)]
    return [strs]
stress.note: cols kept small so the brute-force oracle stays fast
